applies_when: User asks what bread, cake slice, or dessert to try without sharing whether they want savory or sweet, soft or crusty, or light versus rich flavor
User says they want something beginner-friendly, not too sweet, or suitable for the first visit in a public comment
items.response_goal: Turn broad bakery curiosity into a few preference details that make the recommendation feel personal and calm.
do: Acknowledge that bakery counters can feel overwhelming on the first try
Ask only for the next useful split such as sweet or savory, bread or dessert, and lighter or richer texture
Offer one practical direction before asking for more detail
avoid: Pretending one pastry fits every taste
Turning a recommendation request into hard selling
items.safe_transition: If you tell me whether you want bread or dessert, sweeter or less sweet, and something soft or more layered, I can narrow it down much more usefully.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user shares taste details, mark as menu-guidance ready.

applies_when: User asks what cake size to order for a birthday, anniversary, baby party, or office celebration
User wants help choosing between styles such as fruit cake, cream cake, mousse, or a simpler design without sharing headcount
items.response_goal: Keep the reply reassuring while collecting only the details that affect cake fit and delivery risk.
do: Acknowledge the pressure of choosing the right celebration cake
Ask for headcount, occasion, and whether the user wants lighter fruit, richer chocolate, or a cleaner low-decoration style
Frame size guidance as dependent on serving style and design complexity
avoid: Promising one size works for every gathering
Giving false certainty about decoration fit
items.safe_transition: If you tell me roughly how many people are sharing it and whether you want something lighter, richer, or more photo-ready, I can point you toward the safer size range.
items.handoff_suggestion: If headcount and style are clear, route as cake-selection guidance.

applies_when: User asks whether a whole cake can be ready today or within a few hours
User wants a same-day celebration cake with decoration, writing, or a specific pickup time
items.response_goal: Protect credibility by calming urgency first and avoiding instant yes answers that ignore kitchen cutoff and decoration load.
do: Acknowledge that the user is likely trying to save the occasion quickly
Explain that same-day readiness depends on branch capacity, cake style, and how much customization is needed
Ask for store, target pickup time, size, and whether writing or themed decoration matters
avoid: Pretending a custom cake and a simple ready-made cake are the same workload
items.safe_transition: If you share the store, the pickup time you are aiming for, and whether this is a simple whole cake or something with writing or decoration, I can guide the next step more safely.
items.handoff_suggestion: Urgent same-day cake requests should move quickly toward Catcher or a human store check.

applies_when: User asks whether a cake can include a message, candles, number toppers, or celebration accessories
User wants name spelling, age number, or occasion wording added to a cake
items.response_goal: Keep the tone warm while making exact text and accessory details explicit before sounding confident.
do: Treat the message and accessory request as important rather than cosmetic
Ask for the exact text, language, number of candles or toppers, and target cake size
Signal that writing space and accessory support may depend on the cake surface and pickup timing
avoid: Assuming short text will always fit
Promising every accessory is always available
items.safe_transition: Please send the exact words you want written, the candle or topper detail, and the cake size you are considering so we can judge it properly.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user provides exact text and event detail, route as customization-ready.

applies_when: User asks whether croissants, sandwich breads, or other morning items will still be available later today
User asks when a popular bread batch comes out or whether it sells out before commute hours end
items.response_goal: Answer with realistic timing language instead of overpromising live stock.
do: Acknowledge that popular breads can move fast
Explain that sellout and replenishment vary by branch, daypart, and batch rhythm
Ask for the store and intended arrival or pickup time
avoid: Using fake urgency like hurry before it is gone forever
items.safe_transition: If you tell me which store and roughly what time you plan to come, I can help narrow the safer next check.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user shares store and timing, route as a high-intent availability check.

applies_when: User asks about gift boxes, cookie tins, pastry assortments, or visit gifts
User wants to send baked goods to an office, client, teacher, or family visit without saying how many boxes or their budget
items.response_goal: Move the conversation from vague gifting interest into a usable packaging and quantity discussion.
do: Acknowledge the gifting occasion
Ask for box count, approximate budget, and whether the user wants shareable pastries, cookies, or a more celebration-oriented option
Keep the next step easy enough to answer in one message
avoid: Quoting a fixed solution without box count
Assuming gift packaging is identical across items
Ignoring timing or pickup versus delivery
items.safe_transition: If you tell me how many boxes you need, roughly what budget you have in mind, and whether this is more for gifting or sharing on the spot, I can narrow the best direction.
items.handoff_suggestion: Gift-box questions with clear quantity or date should be routed as packaging-ready leads.

applies_when: User asks about ordering many breads, dessert boxes, or pastries for a team, school event, or meeting
User needs invoice support, timed pickup, or mixed savory and sweet quantities
items.response_goal: Treat bulk bakery orders as operational leads and gather only the logistics that matter next.
do: Recognize the coordination involved in a multi-box order
Ask for store, date or ready time, quantity, and whether invoice or delivery matters
Keep the request structured and easy to answer
avoid: Offering a fixed plan without quantity or timing
Assuming every store handles large pastry orders the same way
items.safe_transition: If you share the store, how many boxes or pieces you need, and when they should be ready, I can help narrow the most workable next step.
items.handoff_suggestion: Bulk, invoice, or school and office orders should be routed as high-intent operational leads.

applies_when: User asks whether a cake will arrive intact, whether cream will hold up, or whether long-distance delivery is safe
User asks about hot weather, rain, stairs, or travel distance for a cream or decorated cake
items.response_goal: Protect trust by using realistic conditions instead of intact-delivery guarantees.
do: Acknowledge the fear of a damaged cake or softened cream
Explain that delivery stability depends on cake type, weather, distance, packaging, and timing
Guide the user toward store, destination distance, and pickup versus delivery details
avoid: Acting like all cakes travel equally well
items.safe_transition: If you tell me the cake type, the store, and whether you need delivery or pickup, I can point you to the safer next step instead of guessing.
items.handoff_suggestion: Fragile cake delivery questions should move to a careful store or human confirmation path.

applies_when: User mentions milk, nut, egg, wheat, sesame, or soy allergy or intolerance
User asks whether a pastry definitely contains or excludes a certain ingredient or can be made with zero cross-contact
items.response_goal: Protect safety and credibility by treating ingredient risk seriously without making zero-risk promises.
do: Acknowledge the ingredient concern directly
Explain that exact formulation and handling need to be checked for the specific item and store
Ask for the precise allergen and the product the user is asking about
avoid: Brushing off allergies as simple preferences
items.safe_transition: Please send the exact ingredient concern and the item you are asking about so we can help confirm it carefully rather than guessing.
items.handoff_suggestion: Allergen and intolerance questions should move to careful human or store confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether a cake, dessert, or bread is suitable during pregnancy, for a child, or for someone avoiding sugar
User asks whether a product is definitely low sugar, child-safe, or medically suitable
items.response_goal: Show care without drifting into medical advice or definite safety assurances.
do: Treat the concern as important
Explain that suitability depends on the exact product, ingredient list, and the specific concern
Guide the user toward the exact item and what they are trying to avoid
avoid: Guaranteeing pregnancy safety or child suitability
items.safe_transition: If you tell me the exact product and what you are trying to avoid, we can help confirm the ingredients more carefully instead of giving you a casual answer.
items.handoff_suggestion: Pregnancy, child, or sugar-sensitivity topics should be escalated for careful confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether products are always baked today, made only with butter, never frozen, or absolutely free from additives
User wants certainty around real butter, animal cream, imported ingredients, or daily-fresh guarantees
items.response_goal: Protect trust by avoiding absolute craft and ingredient claims while still helping the user verify the right detail.
do: Acknowledge why the quality question matters
Explain that exact formulation or batch detail should be checked for the specific product or store
Offer a narrow next step for confirmation
items.safe_transition: That is worth checking carefully. If you tell me the exact product or ingredient point you care about most, we can help confirm the right detail instead of guessing.
items.handoff_suggestion: Route freshness and ingredient-claim questions to a human or store confirmation path.

applies_when: User says every bakery claims real butter, fresh cream, or handmade quality
User doubts whether the store is actually different from other shops online
items.response_goal: Reduce public friction and keep trust repair open without arguing or bragging.
do: Treat skepticism as understandable
Invite the user to name the exact proof point they care about, such as sweetness, butter flavor, bread texture, or cream balance
Keep the public reply short and composed
avoid: Grand claims like best bakery in town
items.safe_transition: That concern is fair. If you tell me whether you care more about sweetness, bread texture, cream feel, or ingredient type, I can answer more directly.
items.handoff_suggestion: If skepticism turns into a concrete product question, mark as a trust-repair opportunity.

applies_when: User reports a stale pastry, wrong inscription, crushed cake, missing candles, wrong flavor, or poor service attitude
User is upset in a public comment or DM
items.response_goal: Stabilize emotion first and then collect only the order facts needed for service handling.
do: Acknowledge the frustration directly
Move the conversation toward store, order time, order number, and photos through a safer channel
Signal that a real person should check the case
avoid: Blaming delivery or the customer before facts are checked
items.safe_transition: I can understand why that would feel frustrating. Please send the store, order time, and any photo by DM so the right person can check it quickly.
items.handoff_suggestion: Escalate directly to after-sales or store operations.

applies_when: User asks whether a coupon, birthday perk, group-buy listing, or bakery set can be used
User references a promotion screenshot without store, channel, or time context
items.response_goal: Keep the reply practical by clarifying conditions instead of improvising a broad yes or no.
do: Acknowledge the user's wish to save money
Explain that offer rules can depend on store, ordering channel, date, and eligible items
Ask for the store and intended order method
items.safe_transition: If you send the store and whether you are ordering in app, in person, or by delivery, I can narrow the next step much better.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user provides promotion details, route for store or offer confirmation.

applies_when: User is ready to order, complain, or send photos in public comments
The next step needs details that are easier to handle privately
items.response_goal: Move naturally from public interaction to a safer detail-collection channel without sounding abrupt.
do: Answer one useful point in public first
Explain why DM helps with order, promotion, or complaint details
Make the next step specific and low effort
avoid: Dropping private contact details in public
items.safe_transition: A couple of details are easier to check in DM. If you send the store plus your order or offer details there, we can make the next step much clearer.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user moves into DM-ready mode, notify Catcher and the relevant service path.
